narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70—well above the typical 3.21 average—and a competitive LSAT score of 36, close to the dataset average of 36.70, you've shown real academic promise that many students aspire to match. Your family comes from a middle-income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 92% of students in this analysis. However, the university's admission model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 41% chance you'll fail the bar—high enough to trigger a rejection, as they prioritize applicants likely to pass on the first try. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, far below the average of 5.53, signaling to the model that your later undergraduate performance placed you in the bottom tier compared to peers. This stood out as the strongest driver, outweighing other strengths in the decision. Next in importance, your LSAT score worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success because it's right in line with what typically leads to passing the bar. Similarly, your above-average undergraduate GPA provided a significant boost, countering the risk by demonstrating consistent early achievement. While these positives narrowed the gap, they couldn't fully offset the concern from your lower Year 3 deciles—both Year 1 at 2 and Year 3 at 1—which highlighted a dip that the model sees as a key red flag for bar outcomes in students with similar backgrounds. Compared to typical students who pass, your profile aligns well on GPA and LSAT but lags notably on these decile measures, tipping the scales. To move forward, consider retaking courses or pursuing a post-baccalaureate program to boost your later academic deciles, as even small improvements there could reshape future predictions. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources tailored for Black students navigating these paths, building on your existing strengths. With your GPA and LSAT as anchors, targeted efforts on performance trends could open doors at other schools open to holistic reviews. Keep pushing—many in your position turn predictions around with focused action.
